root_text.an4.163:2.1: Katamā ca, bhikkhave, dukkhā paṭipadā dandhābhiññā?
root_text.an4.163:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu asubhānupassī kāye viharati, āhāre paṭikūlasaññī, sabbaloke anabhiratisaññī, sabbasaṅkhāresu aniccānupassī;
root_text.an4.163:2.3: maraṇasaññā kho panassa ajjhattaṁ sūpaṭṭhitā hoti.
root_text.an4.163:2.4: So imāni pañca sekhabalāni upanissāya viharati—
root_text.an4.163:2.8: So imesaṁ pañcannaṁ indriyānaṁ muduttā dandhaṁ ānantariyaṁ pāpuṇāti āsavānaṁ khayāya.
root_text.an4.163:2.9: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, dukkhā paṭipadā dandhābhiññā.
root_text.an4.163:3.1: Katamā ca, bhikkhave, dukkhā paṭipadā khippābhiññā?
root_text.an4.163:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu asubhānupassī kāye viharati, āhāre paṭikūlasaññī, sabbaloke anabhiratisaññī, sabbasaṅkhāresu aniccānupassī;
root_text.an4.163:3.3: maraṇasaññā kho panassa ajjhattaṁ sūpaṭṭhitā hoti.
root_text.an4.163:3.4: So imāni pañca sekhabalāni upanissāya viharati—
root_text.an4.163:3.8: So imesaṁ pañcannaṁ indriyānaṁ adhimattattā khippaṁ ānantariyaṁ pāpuṇāti āsavānaṁ khayāya.
root_text.an4.163:3.9: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, dukkhā paṭipadā khippābhiññā.
root_text.an4.163:4.1: Katamā ca, bhikkhave, sukhā paṭipadā dandhābhiññā?
root_text.an4.163:4.2: Idha bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati;
root_text.an4.163:4.3: vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati;
root_text.an4.163:4.4: pītiyā ca virāgā upekkhako ca viharati sato ca sampajāno sukhañca kāyena paṭisaṁvedeti yaṁ taṁ ariyā ācikkhanti: ‘upekkhako satimā sukhavihārī’ti tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati;
root_text.an4.163:4.5: sukhassa ca pahānā dukkhassa ca pahānā pubbeva somanassadomanassānaṁ atthaṅgamā adukkhamasukhaṁ upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an4.163:4.6: So imāni pañca sekhabalāni upanissāya viharati—
root_text.an4.163:4.10: So imesaṁ pañcannaṁ indriyānaṁ muduttā dandhaṁ ānantariyaṁ pāpuṇāti āsavānaṁ khayāya.
root_text.an4.163:4.11: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, sukhā paṭipadā dandhābhiññā.
root_text.an4.163:5.1: Katamā ca, bhikkhave, sukhā paṭipadā khippābhiññā?
root_text.an4.163:5.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vivicceva kāmehi vivicca akusalehi dhammehi savitakkaṁ savicāraṁ vivekajaṁ pītisukhaṁ paṭhamaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati …pe… dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ …pe… tatiyaṁ jhānaṁ …pe… catutthaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.an4.163:5.3: So imāni pañca sekhabalāni upanissāya viharati—
root_text.an4.163:5.7: So imesaṁ pañcannaṁ indriyānaṁ adhimattattā khippaṁ ānantariyaṁ pāpuṇāti āsavānaṁ khayāya.
root_text.an4.163:5.8: Ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, sukhā paṭipadā khippābhiññā.

translation_text.an4.163:1.1: “Mendicants, there are four ways of practice.
translation_text.an4.163:1.5: pleasant practice with slow insight, and
translation_text.an4.163:2.1: And what’s the painful practice with slow insight?
translation_text.an4.163:2.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions,
translation_text.an4.163:2.3: and has well established the perception of their own death.
translation_text.an4.163:2.4: They rely on these five powers of a trainee:
translation_text.an4.163:2.5: faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:2.6: But these five faculties manifest in them weakly:
translation_text.an4.163:2.7: faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:2.8: Because of this, they only slowly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life.
translation_text.an4.163:2.9: This is called the painful practice with slow insight.
translation_text.an4.163:3.1: And what’s the painful practice with swift insight?
translation_text.an4.163:3.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates observing the ugliness of the body, perceives the repulsiveness of food, perceives dissatisfaction with the whole world, observes the impermanence of all conditions,
translation_text.an4.163:3.3: and has well established the perception of their own death.
translation_text.an4.163:3.4: They rely on these five powers of a trainee:
translation_text.an4.163:3.5: faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:3.6: And these five faculties manifest in them strongly:
translation_text.an4.163:3.7: faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:3.8: Because of this, they swiftly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life.
translation_text.an4.163:3.9: This is called the painful practice with swift insight.
translation_text.an4.163:4.1: And what’s the pleasant practice with slow insight?
translation_text.an4.163:4.2: It’s when a mendicant, quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters and remains in the first absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of seclusion, while placing the mind and keeping it connected.
translation_text.an4.163:4.3: As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, they enter and remain in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.
translation_text.an4.163:4.4: And with the fading away of rapture, they enter and remain in the third absorption, where they meditate with equanimity, mindful and aware, personally experiencing the bliss of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous and mindful, one meditates in bliss.’
translation_text.an4.163:4.5: With the giving up of pleasure and pain and the disappearance of former happiness and sadness, they enter and remain in the fourth absorption, without pleasure or pain, with pure equanimity and mindfulness.
translation_text.an4.163:4.6: They rely on these five powers of a trainee:
translation_text.an4.163:4.7: faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:4.8: But these five faculties manifest in them weakly:
translation_text.an4.163:4.9: faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:4.10: Because of this, they only slowly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life.
translation_text.an4.163:4.11: This is called the pleasant practice with slow insight.
translation_text.an4.163:5.1: And what’s the pleasant practice with swift insight?
translation_text.an4.163:5.2: It’s when a mendicant … enters and remains in the first absorption … second absorption … third absorption … fourth absorption …
translation_text.an4.163:5.3: They rely on these five powers of a trainee:
translation_text.an4.163:5.4: faith, conscience, prudence, energy, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:5.5: And these five faculties manifest in them strongly:
translation_text.an4.163:5.6: faith, energy, mindfulness, immersion, and wisdom.
translation_text.an4.163:5.7: Because of this, they swiftly attain the conditions for ending the defilements in the present life.
translation_text.an4.163:5.8: This is called the pleasant practice with swift insight.
translation_text.an4.163:5.9: These are the four ways of practice.”

variant_text.an4.163:2.2: paṭikūlasaññī → paṭikkūlasaññī (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed) | anabhiratisaññī → anabhiratasaññī (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed)
